name: M.S. in Biostatistics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 15 courses form a tightly focused, well-ordered biostatistics spine: foundations in MATH 662 Probability Distributions and MATH 665 Statistical Inference lead to health-applied methods (663 Intro Biostatistics, 644 Regression, 763 GLM, 659 Survival Analysis, 654 Clinical Trials, 787 Non-Parametric) and culminate in advanced theory at MATH 786 Large Sample Theory (deepestPrereqChain 4). The one coherence caveat is that the measure-theoretic 768 -> 786 branch is quite theoretical for a professional M.S. and jumps in difficulty from the applied core.
dimensions.suggestion: Clarify that the measure-theoretic MATH 768 -> 786 branch is an advanced/PhD-track option distinct from the applied biostatistics core so students are not surprised by the difficulty jump.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy and well-formed: missingCourseCount is 0 and deepestPrereqChain is 4 (e.g., MATH 786 requires 665 and 768), with explicit sensible gates throughout (665 requires 662; 763 requires 662 and 665). The depth-4 chain sits at the upper edge for a 30-credit M.S. and hinges on the demanding MATH 768 Probability Theory.
dimensions.suggestion: Schedule the MATH 768 -> 786 sequence so students can complete it within a two-year M.S., or mark it optional relative to the applied core.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the biostatistics competencies a graduate should demonstrate are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as designing and analyzing clinical trials, modeling time-to-event data, and communicating results to clinical collaborators.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is 30 (a conventional M.S. total) with all substantive courses at 3 credits, implying roughly ten courses; however requirementGroups is 0 so the required-core vs. elective split is not visible and creditReconcileDelta is null.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a plan of study distinguishing the required biostatistics core (e.g., 663, 665, 654, 659, 763) from electives so the 30 credits are transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative and the titles clearly convey the biostatistics focus (Survival Analysis, Clinical Trials Design and Analysis, Generalized Linear Models), but there are no plan-of-study groups, no labeled core/electives, and no stated distribution.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a plan-of-study table with labeled core and elective courses and the intended term sequence.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is highly relevant to pharma/clinical-research careers: survival analysis (659), clinical trials across phases 1-4 (654), GLM/GEE (763), non-parametrics (787), and DOE (699). Gaps relative to modern biostatistics include the absence of a statistical-computing course in this track (MATH 660 SAS/R appears in the applied-stats program but not here) and no bioinformatics/genomics or Bayesian-trials content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required statistical-computing (R/SAS) course to the biostatistics track and a statistical-genomics or Bayesian-clinical-trials elective.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are substantive and specific rather than placeholder, conveying concrete methods (e.g., MATH 659 on censoring/truncation and hazard functions, MATH 654 on phase 1-4 trial designs and randomization) across the 15 courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit course codes with sensible, well-formed gates and no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0), e.g., MATH 665 requires 662 and MATH 786 requires 665 and 768; the frequent 'or departmental approval' clauses are clear and reasonable.

missingTopics.topic: Statistical computing (R/SAS) as a required course
missingTopics.rationale: The biostatistics sample lacks MATH 660 Statistical Computing with SAS and R that the sibling applied-statistics program includes; hands-on computing is essential for biostatisticians.
MATH 763 covers GLM/GEE, but repeated-measures health data warrant a dedicated longitudinal/mixed-models course.
High-dimensional omics data are central to modern biostatistics, yet no genomics/high-dimensional-biology course appears among the 15.
Study design, confounding, and causal estimands are core to health research but are not represented in the course list.
Bayesian and adaptive designs are increasingly standard in trials and would extend MATH 654 Clinical Trials Design and Analysis.

emergingTrends.trend: Bayesian adaptive and platform trial designs
emergingTrends.rationale: Modern trial methodology is moving toward adaptive/Bayesian designs, a natural modernization of MATH 654.
Regulatory acceptance of real-world evidence makes observational-data and missing-data methods increasingly important.
Omics-scale data are reshaping biostatistics and are absent from the current curriculum.
Predictive modeling with proper calibration and validation is a growing complement to the program's inference-focused core.

emergingTrends.trend: Causal inference for observational health data
emergingTrends.rationale: Causal methods are now central to epidemiology and comparative-effectiveness research.

topSuggestions: Add a required statistical-computing (R/SAS) course to the biostatistics track (currently MATH 660 is not in this program's course set).
Add longitudinal/mixed-models and statistical-genomics electives to modernize the methods coverage.
Publish program-level learning outcomes for the degree.
Publish a plan of study distinguishing the required core from electives and clarifying the theoretical 768 -> 786 branch.
Incorporate Bayesian/adaptive-trial and causal-inference content, extending MATH 654.
